speaker: interviewee
text: Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. This is December 12. I'm interviewing Erna Hilfstein. You were talking about the beating that you received.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, next day, I came in, and I look like a monster. But something kept me going. And I came in, and again, I was-- of course I was working much slower now. I was working. And again, he came in, and he beat me up. Then the German. And I started running away. And I was going to the attic in one of the houses where I was supposed to take some of the pots and pans, and the German went after me, and he really beat me up. And now I was half conscious. But something kept me going. And so I came back to camp, and I was looking absolutely inhuman. And my mother said, you're not going to work. I said, no, I'm going to work. And if he finishes me off, you stay alive, try to-- my mother was still a young woman-- and you will do something in case you survive. I came in, and at that day came the big boss, Bulb, who liked me. He looked at me. He didn't say anything, but I know that he said something to Tzuchsberger. He didn't know who else beat me up, but the beatings stopped. And, in the meantime, Max was also working on this place. A few days before, they cut off some of the columns, and they were supposed to send them to a place, Stalowa Wola, and [NON-ENGLISH], the other place. And whoever was in good graces of the German came back. But somebody said, I think the German-- somebody said they were very jealous because Max was working in-- in this barber beauty shop. So they said probably that we were making-- I think somebody told me that they were saying that we were joking about this guy's head. I went, and I knew that he was making the list of people to get them out from that transport. So I came to him, and I went on my knees, and I cried. And he said, too late. The list went out. Luckily, my husband has a cousin by exactly the same name, much older, who was in charge of the furrier department, and he was very well liked by the Germans, and he managed to get my husband out. When I saw him, we looked like a sight, because one guy, by the name of Colonel [INAUDIBLE], or [INAUDIBLE], incidentally, he was hanged after the war, when he came to Krakow. Beat him up so one half of his face was completely black. And I was, of course, smashed completely. I recovered, and I knew that [INAUDIBLE] will not hit me anymore, because otherwise the German would be on his case. So I told him, when I meet you after the war, in case I survive and you survive, you're not going to live much longer. And he survived. But that was another story, that, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Max was working also for-- was a messenger boy for a German who was in charge of all those factories which were in Krakow still, and he liked him very much. It was supposed to be a very big transport because the Russians were approaching Krakow. That was already 1944, and Max asked whether I could also stay. And the guy-- he asked about my mother. So he said, you also want your mother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I said, I'm going, of course, with my mother. But my mother said, don't go. Because when I come in, they always ask if somebody knows typing, steno, this and that, but you don't have those skills. Therefore, you-- you are in danger, and I am not. And she went to Auschwitz, and that's exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: They asked, who can do this and that? They try her, and instead of going to Auschwitz Birkenau, which was the-- she went to Auschwitz [INAUDIBLE], and she had more food and was working in an office. And I stayed over there. [INAUDIBLE]. And I got job in [INAUDIBLE] which was they were making nice clothes for the top Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, I was very bright, and the woman who was in charge of it, her name was Mrs. Goodman, didn't like that. So she took me, and I had to start fire in the offices of all the Germans over there. There was 11 offices. And each one of them was giving me either piece of paper or something and said, start fire with this. And if I didn't, they were hitting you. But after a while, they were not-- they weren't hitting me too much. So, but somehow, I became such an expert in lighting this with just minimal amount of things which you need to start fire, that it was OK. And however, one day, one German who, like my husband, he was a big killer, he wanted to punish him. So he called me, and he asked him. He never called my husband Hilfstein, but Hilfswine, which means like swine is a pig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said that he should call me. And when I came in, he ask him, is this [GERMAN] frau? He said, jawohl Herr [GERMAN]. He said, [GERMAN]. And he smacked me in my face that I went to another side of the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he said to come back, and then he smacked me on the other side. My face was swollen like this. And Max stood, and he couldn't do anything. He felt like killing the guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to myself, my God. Why? Why this happens? Incidentally, they caught the guy, I think, in Munich after the war, and he also hung. His name was Jahn. And so that was another experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was already-- when about 300 men and 150 women stayed, and they actually burned all the barracks. And, what they did, that's another thing. Since the Russians were coming, they were digging out all the dead bodies over there in the camp and burning them. And one day, they needed more people, so they took also me. And the smell of that burning bodies stays with me forever. And what my husband is upset, I don't like grey hair. Because one woman which we dug out that day had grey hair, and we put her in something before we put her in fire, and her hair, gray hair was hanging, and since then I-- I-- but I-- some kind of aversion to gray hair. I always had it. And I said to my husband, even if I lose every single hair on my head, which are falling out now, I would still do this, because I cannot stand gray hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. Continue? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: One [? sec. ?] OK. Do you want to continue in terms of what happened next, when I think you're going to be transferred soon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Russians were already very, very close, and there were no trains, so we had to go to Auschwitz by foot. And it was winter, that was January, and snow was on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you-- this was in Plaszow then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow, we called it. We can call it Krakow, Plaszow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we walked to Auschwitz. At nighttime, we were sleeping in some stable, and my feet froze. I had, actually, boots, but my feet were frozen. And I felt that I couldn't walk. And then what they going to do to us in Auschwitz? They're probably going to kill us. So I laid down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you know at that time what Auschwitz was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, at that time we already knew, although I knew that my mother was alive in Auschwitz [INAUDIBLE], but she could not help me much. So I-- oh, by the way, this guy who made my Aryan papers, they caught him eventually, and he was in our camp. He came to me, we are going at nighttime through the streets. There were few also Ukrainians around, as they would also disgusted, because they knew when the Russians going to come, and they collaborated whichever, they're going to kill them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came, and he said, run away with me. And I said, I can't because it might happen what happened in Warsaw, you see. We knew that Warsaw, the Russians were by the city when there was the uprising. And then they saw that the Poles going to liberate themselves, so they moved away. And then, of course, a lot of people in the concentration camp, the Poles, and so on, and the Jews, of course, all were killed. Well, at any rate, so I felt that let me go to Auschwitz. And I said, no. I will not run away. But then next day, I-- I couldn't walk anymore, so I lay down, and I said, shoot me. I don't want to live. I cannot take it. The guy, his name was Shupke, came and asked, who will pick her up? So there was a guy, I didn't know it was my husband's cousin. I don't think my husband recognized him. He picked me up, put me around his neck. I-- of course, I weighed not much, and he carried me to Auschwitz. By the time we came to Auschwitz, Auschwitz was already in an uproar because the Russians were very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- the ovens were-- they were actually dismantling everything. I didn't meet my mother because I was in this Birkenau Auschwitz [GERMAN], and she was sitting over there in office, burning all the papers. And people were telling me, she's over there and [INAUDIBLE]. And we stayed three days. They didn't give us the numbers or anything. They didn't have it in their mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the death walk take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this-- this was not the original death walk. This was about three days. But then, there was the death march, which we went from Auschwitz until [NON-ENGLISH]. Men went different road, but in the beginning, the same. And we are going after them. And whenever I saw a dead body on the side, because a lot of them couldn't walk, they were shot, or they just died by themselves. I was looking at every single one of them to see whether it isn't my husband, and it was a nightmare. But I recovered, believe it or not. Those three days, my feet were OK. So I walk until [NON-ENGLISH], where they put us on open train. It was snowing on us, which was good, because we had at least a little bit of water. Although, very little from snow. You cannot really drink much, but at least it was something. But we weren't too cold because you were squeezed so much that we were warming each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We steal from Krakow. I had a whole loaf of bread, which some Hungarian girls took away from me. Not that I didn't fight for it. When we came to Weimar, that's the famous city because of [INAUDIBLE]. The Ukrainians which were watching us were already by themselves disgusted, and I saw a woman, if they tell-- Germans say that they didn't know what was going on, they lie. There were plenty of Germans over there in that depot, and I saw a woman with a girl about my age, and I asked her for a little water. And she looked at me and turned and walked away. She could have given to me. Then they took us from Weimar-- it's very short distance-- to Buchenwald. And Buchenwald didn't have a place for us. So they took us to Bergen-Belsen. And my mother was in the same transfer, but different one. I didn't find her. She went to Ravensbruck. So we didn't meet until well after the war. We came to Bergen-Belsen, and there were only crematoria over there for somebody dead, and there was so many people dying that there were piles of dead bodies. If you go to Holocaust Museum in Washington, the English made some movies when they took over our camp, so you will see those piles over there because they couldn't manage to-- to burn everybody. People were dying like-- you could not be alive when you were healthy longer than three months. It just so happened I came at the end of January, and we were freed at April 15 by the English army of General Montgomery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So although I was so ill, if they would have come two days later, I would have been dead. And Bergen-Belsen was another nightmare. However, I was very lucky. I managed to get myself a job in a German kitchen, which means I had more soup. And I could eat raw potatoes, and turnips, and some carrots, so I had, at least, something in me that kept me alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until one day I found that I had some lice, and then I know that I'm going to get another type of typhoid that was the-- what we called in German, [GERMAN]. From itch, really, I was dying. And, at that time, we were already surrounded by the English, and the camp was supposed to go up, but they didn't manage.

speaker: interviewer
text: The what was it supposed to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were supposed to-- to kill us all. But, and we had no water for several days. I was dying for water. I couldn't eat. And, as a matter of fact, we had a so-called block [INAUDIBLE] that was a German Jewish woman, Mrs. Frankel, who was arrested after the war. I don't know what they did with her, but she was also a killer. And whoever was dead, everybody else had to-- whoever was next to that person, to drag her out and put on the pile.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she saw that I was dying, and she dragged me out, and they put me. And I was laying on the pile of the dead bodies. And next to me was my mother's friend, Mrs. Korniwo. She was a wife of a dentist, very elegant woman before the war, and she was about 40 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said to me a very thin voice, I can never forget this voice, she said to me, roll down from it, and go back and hide in the barrack, because I have a feeling that you will survive. And we knew that the English were close. And-- and then she died. And I rolled down, and I sneak into the barrack and went under those, what they called [INAUDIBLE]. You know, there was three floors of this. And I lay down and, all of a sudden, I hear a voice, the English are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stuck out my head. I said, how do you know? Dying voice. And they said, because they have different uniforms. I said, probably the Germans got them some new uniforms. But then I saw that there was a flag, of course, with swastika outside and was coming down, and the English flag went up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I knew that I survived the war. And I didn't have a drop of water in me, but I began to cry. I survived the war. It was incredible. And that's-- that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, later on, when I was in college already, one of the professors said, ah, what is a flag. That was also the time when people were burning the flags, this and that. They said, ah, what is a flag? I said, don't say it. To me, it's a sacred thing. Because seeing that flag, this was a symbol of freedom to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And each time, although later on, I did not like the English that much because of what they did in Israel and even before that, their flag is a symbol of freedom for me and always will be. My mother was freed by the Russians, my husband by the French, and I, the English. No Americans in this for me, but they managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened if you didn't roll down from that? What would have happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would have died, because it was freezing. Although it was already April, but it was a very, very bad winter and was still very cold. And I would have died from exposure staying there another night and half a day, I would say. That would have been it. But because I managed to get myself in and was relatively warm. There was so many people inside. So that by itself kept us warmer. And I got a piece of blanket over there, where I was laying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I managed. It was as incredible as it was because I really, at that point, I lost my hope to survive. But when I saw that, [INAUDIBLE] I also was very badly beaten in Bergen-Belsen, and so I was not in such a great shape at the end. How were you cared for after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a medic Englishman who knew some German. I didn't know a single word of English except yes and no. And he took me outside. They made tents, and he put me on a hammock because I was only bones. He asked me first if I am 7 or 70. He couldn't tell, even, my age. And he put some cotton on it. And then-- well, of course, they sprayed me with something, so immediately, they killed all the lice and everything, because I didn't have lice so long. I was healthy, but then I was laying for more than a week, dying. And then he fed me, every half an hour, a little porridge. If I would have started eating-- like 20,000 people died because they threw themselves at food. And the soldiers gave them food because they-- they were not trained, and they should have known. You see, this was a very grave error that they gave normal food, and people who are starved, start eating, developed tremendous diarrhea and died. Because of the porridge, I survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was one of the few lucky ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any specific friend or somebody who you related to during all these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that camp, I became friendly with one girl, and we kept for quite a while together. And then two more friends, but when you are that sick-- but we really, I might say that we formed a very fine friendship. And so long I stayed in Bergen-Belsen, we were friends. And after the war, somehow, I met them once or twice later on. But not much. But we were friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon-- now, very soon, they burned the whole camp. They shouldn't have. They should have left part of it, so the world would see how it was like. They transfer us not far away, where it was called for the German [INAUDIBLE]. People were coming from the Russian front. They were staying over there. They were very nice building, very comfortable, so they put us over there. And they were giving us a certain amount of food and also some medical care, and I was able to go to a dentist. My teeth were in very bad shape because-- although, we had a dentist in Krakau-Plaszow, I wouldn't go to them because they-- the job was when they killed someone, to come out and remove all the gold teeth. So there were using this kind of utensils. I wouldn't-- I somehow couldn't take that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after-- now as soon as I started recovering, in the beginning, I couldn't even walk upstairs. I was going on all four. I wanted to know whether I am all alone or whether my mother and my husband survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people start already travelling and coming over there. And the guy who knew Max came in. He found his wife. And I came over there, and he told me that my husband was in Flossenburg and that they hanged him. I came in, I said well, I don't know why I survived, really, but maybe my mother is alive. And then one lady came, and I knew that if my mother survived from Ravensbruck, she would be in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friends were living together in that place, told me, go and find out. I said, I'm afraid. Because if I would find out that I don't even have a mother, I don't know why I survived. Say, you better go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, and I opened the door. And the lady saw me and said, oh my God, Erna, if your mother knew, she would have been here with me. So then I went back, and I said, I am not an orphan. And we were-- every day, one of us was cleaning that place, keeping it spotless. And it was not my day. I said, let me wash the floor. Let me clean. That was my first reaction that I have a mother. So I said, now I have to hitchhike to to Poland. And I start preparing myself for it. By preparation, I mean they were giving us cigarettes, and of course, I wasn't smoking. So I have those cigarettes, because that was actually means of-- you could buy anything. Not for marks, but for cigarettes. And also, in order to have-- as soon as I recover a little bit, I was taking cigarettes from my friends, and with one other girl, a little younger than I-- we were living together. Her name was Lucy. We're going to the little villages, and we were selling the cigarettes for food. Then I was giving part of it to the people who gave me cigarettes and part for keeping for us. So we had really very good food. We had cheeses, we have some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kielbasas and whatever. We had-- and we had fruit. Because, otherwise, people were getting some food. Well, the English didn't have much, but at that time also, something happened, which I wasn't speaking much before, but now I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: The English-- and that was my problem with the English, you see. General Montgomery, unlike the generals in America, had forbidden the English soldiers to associate with German girls. Since they didn't have the wives or girlfriends or anybody, with whom they going to go out? With the girls from concentration camp who are recovering nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, so, I didn't know about it until one day, I went in the middle of this little town over there, and I see some jeeps and English soldiers who knew German, were saying, come on, girls. You're going to go to a party, you're going to dance, and you're going to have good sandwiches, ice cream. I run back to our place, and I said-- the other girl, they were two sisters, and one of them was still very sick. And I said, Lucy, let's go. We're going to have sandwiches, ice cream. I don't remember how ice cream and chocolate, everything tastes. So, let's-- so she said, terrific. We go over there, and we go. There were several jeeps. We go to that place where the soldier was staying. And, of course, there is a party. There is music, dances which we didn't know, Hokey Pokey, and Lambeth Walk and Boops a Daisy. We got to dance first. I ate these magnificent sandwiches, and I ate ice cream. I drank some sodas. I felt like a human being. And then, I'm dancing. Lucy knew English because her father was an engineer, and they were in New Mexico. He was building something before the war. So she learned English. And she said to me, Erna whatever they will say to you, you say no. I said, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guy who took me to dance, first he-- I'll never forget this. When it comes to this bumps, he bumped me here, and I was still very skinny. I run into the wall. I said, oh, well. And then when the next bumps came, I bumped him that he went into the wall. And was very nice. Then he said something to my ear, and I said no.

speaker: interviewee
text: After, somebody else asked me to dance, and I said, Lucy, you know, let's go to the bathroom. I cannot take it. I ate already, I had my chocolate, I had ice cream. That's it. Going to the bathroom, and the girls are coming in with a lot of English cigarettes. And-- not the German horrible kind which were given to us. And I said to them, how--
